template.name: About Here / video-essay template

structure.job: Personal stake or surprising fact tied to a place/problem
Name the city/system; why viewer should care now
History, data, maps, interviews, dry humor as tension relief
Reframe — policy failure, human cost, or 'what nobody talks about'
One clear takeaway + soft CTA; avoid preachy moralizing

farsi_adaptation: Open in Farsi with a Vancouver-specific image (SkyTrain, rent board, a street name)
Keep numbers/maps on screen — Farsi voiceover over English B-roll works for diaspora + locals
Humor: observational, not meme-heavy; About Here uses understatement
End with 'اگر تازه واردی' / newcomer angle when relevant

samples.title: The $2BN Megaproject Under Vancouver
samples.hook_30s: There’s a huge underground construction project happening in Canada that’s set to transform one of its largest cities. As Vancouver prepares for a population boom in the coming years, midway through a housing crisis, a $2BN new subway line is in progress. When complete, it’ll kick-start a massive urban development plan — a whole new downtown — and these teams are going to extreme lengths to get it done.
samples.act_25: So, what exactly is going into this new project? Well, it consists of six new underground stations, five kilometres of twin-bored tunnels and an elevated guideway — or viaduct — joining the new line to the existing infrastructure. This 700-metre new connection is made up of 21 pillars and more than 100 concrete beams — each one 30 metres long and weighing almost 40 tonnes. Following completion, the guideway will take passengers from VCC-Clark — where the Millennium Line currently terminates abov
samples.act_50: place these rings behind it — thousands of them — to make the tunnel lining. They’re sent through the tunnel using special vehicles before they’re picked up by a crane and placed on a conveyor belt, which takes them up to the machine. The segments are then arranged in a full ring, which the machine uses to propel itself forward and carry on burrowing, travelling up to 18 metres each day. At one point, Phyllis had to dig down to a depth of 22 metres. Why? So it can pass underneath the other subwa
samples.act_75: and pushed back completion of the whole scheme from 2025 to 2026. Once that happens, Vancouverites can expect to see the skyline around Broadway get significantly taller, as construction on the so-called Broadway Plan finally begins. That’s Vancouver’s proposal to build a ‘second downtown’ by replacing many of the low-rise buildings with high-rises. The result would be more space for homes and businesses, but the effect that would have on people already living and working here has become another

samples.title: A quick tour of the UBC Vancouver campus
samples.hook_30s: Welcome to UBC, our campus is pretty big so let's go check it out. This is the Musqueam Post. It marks one of the main entrances to campus. UBC is situated on the unceded, ancestral territory of the Musqueam people. Now we have the UBC Bookstore where you can buy your textbooks, get your UBCcard, UBC merch, and even grab a coffee. The big building on your right is the Nest, which is owned and run by students through the Alma Mater Society. It's the student hub on campus where you can get a bite to eat, set up a bank account, and find a nice spot to study.
samples.act_25: Here's Brock Hall, the home of Enrolment Services. You can pay your tuition here and chat with advisors about awards and scholarships. Did you know that UBC is over 400 hectares in size? That's as big as 750 football fields. It's also a popular filming location. You might recognize it in TV shows and movies such as She's the Man, X-Men Origins Wolverine, and Battlestar Galactica. Now we've arrived at one of UBC's most iconic sites. The flagpole is situated just above the Rose Garden—a spectacula
samples.act_50: On the right is Koerner Library. It's actually shaped like a book, open to the spine and facing down. Do you see it? Come here to get some serious quiet studying in. Next is the Irving K. Barber Learning Centre—one of the top study spots on campus with bookable rooms and spacious desks. To our left is the Chemistry Building, one of the most distinctive buildings on campus. Science students will spend a lot of time at labs and lectures here. This is actually the oldest academic building on campus
samples.act_75: That giant blue whale skeleton in the window is one of only 21 in the world. Now we have the Earth and Ocean Sciences Building where future geologists study rocks. Visit the Pacific Museum of the Earth to see some cool stones. This is the Engineering Cairn. Throughout the year, you might notice that it gets painted by different student groups. Coming up on the left is the Institute for Computing Information and Cognitive Systems. This is where Computer Science students take classes. Now we've ar

samples.title: Vancouver’s Transit Is in Trouble...
samples.hook_30s: This is 49th and Cambie in Vancouver. And you might not know it with all the cars, but it actually hosts a busy bus route and one stop of the famous Sky Train. But there's a bit of a problem. The whole system it represents is currently facing a transit death spiral. The transit death spiral is a pretty simple idea. It's where a drop in ridership causes fair revenue to fall, prompting service cuts that make transit less convenient,
samples.act_25: they've shifted service away from Vancouver and into Surrey. They were doing this because there was really big overcrowding in Surrey and demand was dropping in Vancouver. But that has created bigger drops in Vancouver. And so today what we see is that while other parts of the region have recovered uh in terms of ridership back to pre-COVID levels in these parts of Vancouver where service has been cut aggressively, ridership has not recovered. It's kind of plateaued at like 50 60% of what it was

samples.title: Metro Vancouver and Gunter's Chain
samples.hook_30s: We live in a world created by the early surveyors, where they placed their posts were final. Even mistakes made on the US Border remain legally binding. Once established, street systems are resilient. They have survived nuclear bombs and catastrophic fires. Edmund Gunter standardized the measurement of land. Gunter's sixty six foot chain became the measure of our urban world. Vancouver roads are one or one and a half chains wide.
samples.act_25: used on CPR properties. Royal Engineer Colonel Moody anchored our region on the Coast Meridian where the ocean meets the 49th parallel. He insisted cities be north of the Fraser River to defend against the US. He built New Westminster as the capital then North Road and Kingsway to supply it. He set aside military reserves which became UBC Stanley Park, Jericho Park, Central Park. He laid out Hastings Townsite to Nanaimo Street. Fraser Street connected the Fraser River. His Granville government r
samples.act_50: anchor the new Vancouver. Six square mile townships created baselines and range lines that form borders of our cities like North Road and Boundary Road. Colonel Moody laid out the skeleton of the region and was the first of many important Vancouver planners. Gunter had reconciled the ancient measures based on 4 with the newer ones based on 10. In Gunter's World each chain has 4 rods but also one hundred links. 10 chains make a furlong which an ox could plow before resting. Eight furlongs make a
samples.act_75: Colonel Moody made each street and avenue in the Fraser Valley one furlong, every eighth makes one mile. In Richmond he made the distance between Number 1 Road and Number 2 Road one mile. Throughout Western Canada 6 square mile townships make 36 sections of 640 acres. 4 quarter sections of 160 acres. 40 acre legal subdivisions and 10 acre quarter legal subdivisions creating a street system great for farming, but difficult to build a city on. We live in the world these early measures created they

samples.title: Every Insanely Well Designed Cities Explained in 9 Minutes (Part 3)
samples.hook_30s: Vienna builds 220,000 affordable apartments while most cities argue about zoning regulations. This Austrian capital proves public housing doesn't mean drab concrete blocks from the 1970s. The city owns 220,000 social housing units that house 500,000 residents. These aren't slums. They're mixed-income communities with courtyards, playgrounds, and ground-floor shops. Sixty-two percent of Vienna's population qualifies for social housing because eligibility extends up to ?60,900 annual income. That's not poverty housing. That's actual middle-class policy. The
samples.act_25: nine regular blocks and bans through-traffic from the interior streets. Cars can only access the perimeter roads at crawling speeds. The interior becomes pedestrian paradise with playgrounds, cafe seating, and actual trees. The pilot project in Poblenou reduced traffic noise by 82%. Air quality improved so dramatically that asthma rates dropped 24% among young residents living inside superblocks. Local businesses initially protested the car restrictions. Then foot traffic increased 30% and reven
samples.act_50: 30 kilometers of climate-controlled tunnels linking office towers, shops, and subway stations. Over 200,000 workers use PATH daily to avoid Canadian winter. The underground network houses 1,200 businesses generating $2 billion in annual retail sales. The result is accidentally brilliant car-free urbanism. No traffic lights, no exhaust fumes, just pedestrians buying coffee underground. Toronto also invested $5.3 billion expanding the subway and light rail network. The Eglinton Crosstown LRT adds
samples.act_75: their mistakes. Frankfurt's real genius shows in transit infrastructure. The city operates Europe's busiest railway station moving 450,000 passengers daily. High-speed trains connect Frankfurt to Paris in four hours and Berlin in four hours. The S-Bahn suburban rail system carries 370,000 daily commuters across the metro region. Frankfurt added 120 kilometers of protected bike lanes since 2010. Cycling mode share increased from 9% to 18% in a decade. The city provides 12,000 bike parking spaces

samples.hook_30s: Hey, it's Daniel Lee, project coordinator here at Jjang Architect. And today I want to walk you through something that sounds super technical, actually affects almost everyone living in Vancouver. It's called the official development plan or ODP. And yeah, I get it. It doesn't sound very exciting, but trust me, this is something that's going to shape how the city grows for the next 30 years. And whether you're a renter, homeowner, investor, builder, or just
samples.act_25: Vancouver has to turn the Vancouver plan into a legally binding ODP by June 2026. And that's where we are now. The city's in the middle of that process. The first draft of the ODP will be released in the late 2025 and once approved, it will become law. So what's actually in this plan? At the core, it's built around the three big ideas. More housing options, a stronger local economy, and a healthier environment. Let's start with housing. Right now, a huge portion of Vancouver is zoned for single
samples.act_50: transportation, restoring ecosystems, and protecting big natural areas like parks, trees, and waterways. The goal is to make the city more resilient while reducing emissions at the same time. You've already seen these ideas in action in residential areas. The city already approved new zoning rules that allows for more multiplexes, even on traditional single family lots. The new R1-1 zone, for example, you can now build up to six strata units or eight secured rental homes on one lot. In addition
samples.act_75: corridors and the sky train lines that are changing. Areas like downtown, Cami, and even future plans in places like Rupert and Renfruit are also in the spotlight. And let's not forget about the Broadway Plan, which is pretty controversial. It was officially approved by the city council in 2022. The Broadway Plan covers a huge area from Clark Drive to Vine Street and from 1 Avenue all the way down to 16th. Now, that's around 500 city blocks. The city's vision is ambitious. samples.title: Inside Vancouver’s plan to be the greenest city in the world
samples.hook_30s: This is Vancouver, one of the largest cities in Canada. Ten years ago it made a bold promise: Vancouver will be the greenest city in the world by 2020. Good evening Vancouver You ready to roll? Now plenty of other cities have climate plans. Even in america around half of large cities have plans to cut emissions, usually by some date far off in the future. But Vancouver's plan was different.
samples.act_25: In many ways, Vancouver seemed like the perfect city to do this. Most of the city's electricity came from renewable hydropower, so its carbon footprint was quite small for a city its size. Just look at how Vancouver compared to Portland, which is actually quite similar in population, wealth, and crunchy reputation. But that advantage was a double-edged sword. It meant that Vancouver had already gotten rid of the easy carbon emissions. The remaining emissions were mostly from buildings and transp
samples.act_50: This bike lane has been installed at considerable taxpayer expense over the objections of thousands. But once the bike lanes were finished, city officials said the anger mostly blew over. And bike ridership went up! In the end, Vancouver didn't only meet their transportation goals, they exceeded them! Cutting car miles by 37%, and getting walking, biking, or transit to make up 54% of total trips. Cutting building emissions proved to be a bit trickier. Vancouver needed to cut these by 20%. Most o
samples.act_75: And that left a second option: Vancouver could write its own standards , which would mean picking a fight with the fossil fuel industry. And that's what they ended up doing. In 2018 , after a long battle with the local gas utility, the city finally rolled out new building standards. But it was too late for those changes to count much toward that 2020 deadline. Big picture? Vancouver made climate progress. It cut its emissions by 9%. But it fell short of its overall climate goal. Vancouver's loss
